5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

In my last post, I talked about the mindset shift required to build functional web applications without a traditional background in code. But let’s be real, high-level philosophy doesn’t deploy apps.
If you are building an ecosystem with real commercial goals, you eventually have to confront infrastructure. You have to handle domain routing, database schemas, environment variables, hosting latency, and SEO indexing.
When I set out to build bizdocs.lk, celebrationstudio.lk, and my upcoming wedding planning application, I didn’t just want to build a few UI mockups. I wanted a robust, scalable, modern stack.
Here is the exact technical blueprint of how I orchestrated a production environment using a unified stack of Google Antigravity and VS Code agents, with GitHub serving as the central nervous system connecting the entire operation.
The Infrastructure Matrix
Instead of writing custom scripts to bridge services, I used my agent workflow to configure a modern, industry-standard serverless stack. By using natural language to direct the agents, I spun up the following architecture:
1. Identity & Branding: .lk Domain Registry

• Role in Ecosystem: Establishes local trust and a solid professional presence in Sri Lanka.

• Agent Configuration Method: Managed via natural language commands to map DNS routing and validate records without touching a traditional networking dashboard.
2. Hosting & CI/CD: Vercel (via GitHub)

• Role in Ecosystem: GitHub acts as the absolute source of truth for the codebase, automatically triggering Vercel to handle global edge network delivery, serverless computing, and automated continuous deployment streams.

• Agent Configuration Method: The agents connected the GitHub repositories directly to Vercel, configuring automated build triggers so that any code updates committed by the agents immediately deploy to production.
3. Backend & Database: Supabase (Postgres via GitHub)

• Role in Ecosystem: Acts as the central relational engine to store data and manage the live inventory tracking for celebrationstudio.lk, seamlessly linked to the frontend project structure via GitHub.

• Agent Configuration Method: Built through natural language prompts to generate relational database schemas, write Row-Level Security (RLS) policies, and allow the coding agents to commit database integrations directly to the GitHub repository.
4. Growth & Monitoring: Google Analytics & Search Console

• Role in Ecosystem: Tracks user behaviour metrics, monitors application drop-off points, and ensures instant SEO indexing for organic discovery.

• Agent Configuration Method: Deployed by directing the agents to inject the Global Site Tag into the framework’s header and serve HTML verification files directly from the public root directory.
1. The Deployment Pipeline: Handling Custom Domains on Vercel via GitHub
For hosting, I put every single project on Vercel by linking it directly to my GitHub repositories. Its global edge network guarantees low latency, and its serverless architecture means I never have to worry about server capacity or manual scaling. Because Vercel continuously deploys straight from GitHub, the AI agents simply push code updates or bug fixes to the repository, and the live site updates automatically in seconds.
The main technical challenge for a non-coder is usually DNS mapping. I secured my domains through the official .lk Domain Registry. To hook this up to Vercel, I didn’t write a single line of networking code. Instead, I let my agents handle the handshake:

• I pointed the agents to the target domain name.

• The agents inspected the Vercel project deployment and explicitly identified the exact A records (pointing to Vercel’s IP 76.76.21.21) and CNAME records required.

• Once I dropped those records into the registry dashboard, the agents monitored the propagation status, automatically provisioned the SSL certificates, and verified that the routing was completely secure.
2. The Backend: Building a Dynamic Inventory System with Supabase
While bizdocs.lk handles core utility logic, celebrationstudio.lk is a party items rental platform. A marketplace like that lives and dies by its database. It needs to know exactly what items are available, track quantities, and handle real-time reservation states.
I chose Supabase as the backend engine. It gives me the full power of a relational Postgres database combined with instant, auto-generated REST APIs.
To bridge the gap between my database and my frontend without writing a line of code, I connected Supabase to my project via GitHub. This created a direct pipeline for my coding agents to interact with the repository. From there, the agent stack acted as my remote database administrator through a highly efficient feedback loop:

• Schema Design via Prompts: I described the business rules in plain English: “Create an inventory table for party rentals where each item has an ID, name, description, category, rental price per day, stock quantity, and an array of image URLs.” The coding agent then generated the exact SQL queries needed to build it, which I simply copied and pasted directly into the Supabase SQL editor.

• Row-Level Security (RLS): Security is non-negotiable. I didn’t need to know how to write complex access policies. The agent handed me the exact security blocks, ensuring that public visitors could read the inventory, but only authenticated admin accounts could modify it. A quick copy-paste later, the database was locked down.

• GitHub Automation & Git Syncing: Once the database foundation was pasted into Supabase, the coding agents took the wheel via GitHub. They read the repository structure, handled the API integrations, and committed the backend logic directly to the codebase.
By utilising this hybrid workflow, letting the agents give me the exact queries to paste into the database dashboard while they handled the code synchronisation through GitHub, I had a fully dynamic, secure inventory system running in record time.
3. The Discovery Loop: Google Analytics & Search Console
An app is completely useless if nobody can find it. The final piece of the technical puzzle was ensuring total visibility.
I needed Google Analytics to understand user flow and drop-off points, and Google Search Console to ensure Google was indexing the pages correctly.
Instead of searching through files to find where to paste tracking snippets, I gave the raw tracking IDs to the agents. They automatically injected the Global Site Tag into the head component of the application framework. For Search Console validation, they dynamically served the HTML verification file right from the public directory. Within minutes of deployment, the tracking loops were live, and data was flowing.
The Technical Reality Check
You do not need to be an expert syntactician to deploy production architecture. You need to understand system relationships. Once you know how a domain talks to a host, how a repository acts as the bridge, and how that host connects to a database, you can use an agent stack to execute the setup flawlessly.
The Result: Enterprise Tech, Zero Code Written
By relying on an agent-first development model, I didn’t just build a website; I built a secure, authenticated, indexed web application stack.
The framework is entirely modern, fully detached from monolithic builders, and highly scalable. If I need to change an inventory structure on celebrationstudio.lk, I change the description in my prompt, the agents update the codebase via GitHub, and Vercel automatically deploys the changes.
The stack is no longer the bottleneck. Your imagination is!
